headline: AI outperforms ER doctors in Harvard diagnostic study
subtitle: OpenAI's o1-preview beats physicians · Pentagon adds 8 AI partners · Claude Design tool launches
intro: An older OpenAI model just demonstrated it can diagnose emergency room patients more accurately than attending physicians in a real-world Harvard study. The findings suggest AI is ready for a more formal clinical role, even as millions already use AI for health advice daily.

bullets.text: OpenAI's o1-preview outdiagnoses ER physicians in Harvard trial
bullets.summary: The 2024-released o1-preview model correctly diagnosed 67.1% of 76 real emergency cases compared to 55.3% and 50.0% for two attending physicians. In one instance, the AI identified a rare flesh-eating infection roughly 12-24 hours before the treating doctor, using only raw electronic health records.

bullets.text: Pentagon adds 8 AI partners while keeping Anthropic blacklisted
bullets.summary: The Department of Defense approved SpaceX, OpenAI, Google, Nvidia, Reflection, Microsoft, AWS, and Oracle for classified networks despite Anthropic facing exclusion. The Pentagon cited Anthropic's Mythos model as a separate security concern amid White House pressure over compute resource allocation.

bullets.text: Claude Design lets you build landing pages with AI variations
bullets.summary: Anthropic's new Claude Design tool generates multiple high-converting landing page mockups through a simple interface. Users describe their product and target audience, provide competitor examples, and Claude creates variations that can be refined through comments and deployed directly to code.

bullets.text: OpenAI ships Codex Pets for tracking agent task progress
bullets.summary: OpenAI released Codex Pets, animated desktop companions that display agent workflow progress without requiring users to switch windows. The feature integrates with existing ChatGPT subscriptions through OpenClaw, representing OpenAI's stance against Anthropic's AI access restrictions.

bullets.text: Maryland becomes first US state to ban AI-driven price discrimination
bullets.summary: Maryland enacted legislation prohibiting retailers from using personalized shopper data for algorithmic price markups, with penalties reaching $25,000 per violation. The law marks the first state-level regulation specifically targeting AI-powered dynamic grocery pricing tactics.

bullets.text: SAG-AFTRA secures AI guardrails in new four-year studio contract
bullets.summary: The actors' union negotiated stronger AI protections in its latest studio agreement, with leadership refusing to finalize terms until Hollywood made meaningful concessions on digital likeness and voice usage protections.

bullets.text: Anthropic prepares Jupiter-v1-p model for potential launch
bullets.summary: Anthropic is testing its Jupiter-v1-p model ahead of what could be a May 6 developer conference launch. The timing suggests the company is preparing a significant announcement around the new model.

bullets.text: Google tests new Omni model for video generation
bullets.summary: Google is experimenting with a new Omni model focused on video generation capabilities ahead of its I/O conference. This reflects the industry's broader push into multimodal AI systems.

bullets.text: OpenAI adds animated pets and config imports to Codex
bullets.summary: OpenAI is expanding Codex with new features including animated pets and configuration import functionality. These additions make the platform more engaging and flexible for users.

bullets.text: Replit CEO discusses competitive dynamics and Cursor
bullets.summary: Replit's Amjad Masad spoke about the company's position relative to Cursor and broader market competition. He discussed why Replit remains independent and confident in its long-term strategy.

bullets.text: Top AI companies agree to Pentagon deals for classified work
bullets.summary: Major AI firms are moving into defense applications with classified government contracts. This signals growing integration of AI capabilities into national security infrastructure.

bullets.text: Anthropic nears $1.5B joint venture with Wall Street
bullets.summary: Anthropic is in talks to create a major joint venture with prominent Wall Street firms for approximately $1.5 billion. This move underscores the financial industry's interest in AI infrastructure.

bullets.text: Cloudflare lets agents provision accounts, domains, and deployments autonomously
bullets.summary: Cloudflare now enables agents to create accounts, subscribe, register domains, and deploy without manual dashboard work. Humans still approve permissions, but the setup process is now readable and executable by AI systems, lowering barriers for non-technical builders.

bullets.text: Stripe launches agentic commerce suite with agent-ready payments and accounts
bullets.summary: Stripe announced new tools including Link wallet for agents, streaming payments, and Treasury accounts designed for agent interactions. The Link CLI gives agents one-time-use payment credentials without exposing real card details, opening commerce automation to non-humans.

bullets.text: Cursor SDK enables building agents with Cursor's runtime and models directly
bullets.summary: Cursor released an SDK letting developers build agents using the same runtime and models powering Cursor itself. This allows embedding agents in CI/CD pipelines, automations, and third-party products like Gmail integrations.

bullets.text: Warp terminal goes open-source with OpenAI as founding sponsor
bullets.summary: Warp released its source code publicly with OpenAI's backing, betting that community contributors provide direction while agents handle implementation. This shifts developer tooling toward AI-assisted iteration.

bullets.text: Lightfield AI-native CRM ships Skills for plain-English workflow automation
bullets.summary: Lightfield launched Skills, letting users define workflows in plain English and execute them via voice commands. Agents execute across the full data graph with code execution, web search, and file I/O capabilities.

bullets.text: Martin Fowler updates coding notes on verification, guardrails, and agentic engineering
bullets.summary: Martin Fowler published updated notes distinguishing between 'vibe coding' and real agentic engineering, including practical guidance on verification and guardrails for AI-assisted development.

bullets.text: AI unearthing bugs faster than industry can patch them
bullets.summary: The UK's National Cyber Security Centre warned of a coming 'patch wave' as AI tools discover software flaws at unprecedented speed and scale. Anthropic's Claude Mythos found over 2,000 previously unknown vulnerabilities across major operating systems, with 99% still unpatched. The agency recommends prioritizing internet-facing systems, enabling automatic updates, and replacing legacy systems that cannot receive patches.

bullets.text: China blocked Meta's acquisition of AI agent startup Manus
bullets.summary: China cited national security concerns over foreign acquisition of advanced technology in denying Meta's deal for the AI agent startup. The move reflects broader scrutiny of foreign tech acquisitions in the country.

bullets.text: OpenAI restricted GPT-5.5-Cyber after mocking Anthropic
bullets.summary: OpenAI announced limits on GPT-5.5-Cyber access to vetted cyber defenders only, adopting the same restricted rollout strategy it had previously criticized Anthropic for using with Mythos. The move highlights the tension between capability and safety in releasing powerful security tools.

bullets.text: Gemini can generate Google Docs, Sheets, PDFs from prompts
bullets.summary: Gemini expanded its capabilities to directly create Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, Excel, CSV, PDF, and Markdown files without copy-pasting. Users can research and document simultaneously, convert receipts to spreadsheets, and create multiple formats at once, though edits currently duplicate rather than modify existing files.

bullets.text: Meta tracking employee keystrokes across apps for AI training
bullets.summary: Meta launched the Model Capability Initiative, which monitors employee keystrokes across hundreds of applications including Google, Slack, and LinkedIn to train internal AI systems. The program represents a significant employee monitoring effort tied to AI model development.

main_story.title: Harvard study shows older AI model surpasses emergency room physicians
main_story.body: A Harvard study published in Science evaluated OpenAI's o1-preview model from 2024 against two attending physicians across 76 real emergency department cases. The AI achieved a 67.1% correct diagnosis rate at initial triage compared to 55.3% and 50.0% for the physicians. Notably, independent reviewers scoring the diagnoses could not distinguish between AI and human-generated assessments. In one remarkable case, the model identified a rare flesh-eating infection in a transplant patient approximately 12 to 24 hours before the treating physician detected it, using only raw electronic health record text.
main_story.why: The findings carry significant implications as millions of patients already consult AI for health guidance daily. If a model several generations behind frontier models already outperforms attending physicians, the potential for advanced AI to improve clinical decision-making in formal healthcare settings becomes increasingly credible.
main_story.take: While the results are compelling, deploying AI into actual emergency departments requires careful implementation, validation, and regulatory oversight. This research suggests AI could serve as a diagnostic aid alongside physicians rather than a replacement, potentially catching cases that might otherwise be missed.

around_the_horn.text: Chinese court rules replacing worker with AI doesn't justify termination
Sam Altman announces ChatGPT subscriptions work within OpenClaw agentic tool
ElevenLabs launches ElevenMusic for AI song generation with creator payouts

previous.headline: UiPath CMO on why most AI projects fail
previous.subtitle: Orchestration over isolation · Job anxiety is nuanced · Where humans stay essential
previous.intro: UiPath's CMO Michael Atalla explains why 70-80% of AI initiatives stall in pilot phase and how enterprises should rethink workflows for AI success, drawing parallels to the cloud computing transition.

bullets.text: From task automation to orchestrated workflows uniting AI agents and people
bullets.summary: UiPath's five-year evolution reflects the shift from isolated task automation to orchestrating how AI, automation, and humans collaborate end-to-end. Atalla compares this to the Office 365 transition, where success required rethinking workflows, not just deploying technology.

bullets.text: Coordination failures cause AI projects to stall after initial pilot success
bullets.summary: Atalla identifies the core barrier: pilots succeed in isolation but organizations lack coordination to scale. Without visibility between tools and alignment with business goals, costs accumulate and ROI vanishes, leaving 70-80% of initiatives stuck.

bullets.text: AI threatens entry-level roles but creates demand for workflow design work
bullets.summary: Job anxiety is justified—entry-level dev positions dropped 20% since 2024. However, routine work transforms rather than disappears, creating new roles in governance and process ownership that require different skills than traditional development.

bullets.text: Agents handle unstructured data and exceptions; humans retain judgment decisions
bullets.summary: Atalla describes a practical division: AI agents excel at document understanding and fraud detection within defined processes, while humans oversee exceptions and approvals. Full autonomy narratives overstate reality; the model keeps humans in governance and accountability roles.

bullets.text: Enterprises expect AI to fix broken processes rather than improve good ones
bullets.summary: The lightning round reveals a common mistake: deploying AI without redesigning underlying workflows. AI amplifies efficiency in solid processes but makes bad workflows more expensive, echoing the broader coordination problem.

bullets.text: Cloudflare lets agents autonomously provision accounts and deploy
bullets.summary: Cloudflare now allows agents to create accounts, purchase domains, obtain API tokens and deploy without manual dashboard interactions. Humans still approve permissions, but the setup process is becoming agent-readable.

bullets.text: Stripe launches agentic commerce suite with wallet for agents
bullets.summary: Stripe announced the Agentic Commerce Suite including a wallet for agents and streaming payments. The new Link CLI provides agents one-time-use payment credentials without exposing real card details, making payments agent-native.

bullets.text: Cursor SDK enables building agents with Cursor's runtime and models
bullets.summary: Cursor released an SDK allowing developers to build agents using the same runtime, harness and models powering Cursor itself. Agents can be embedded in products, run in CI/CD pipelines, or create end-to-end workflow automations.

bullets.text: Lightfield ships Skills for AI-native CRM with plain English workflows
bullets.summary: Lightfield launched Skills, letting users define workflows in plain English that execute against their full data graph. The AI agent handles code execution, web search and file I/O automatically.

bullets.text: Warp terminal goes open-source with OpenAI as founding sponsor
bullets.summary: Warp's terminal became open-source with OpenAI backing the repository. The bet is that human contributors provide direction and verification while agents handle more implementation work.

bullets.text: here.now adds private cloud storage for agents and instant publishing
bullets.summary: here.now launched private cloud storage for agents alongside instant web publishing capabilities. Ben synced 2.5k local files and memories to the platform with a single prompt.

bullets.text: Pi: a 4-tool coding agent powering OpenClaw via simple architecture
bullets.summary: Pi is an open-source coding agent built by Austrian developer Mario Zechner that ships with just four tools: read, write, edit, and bash. Everything else users need—plan mode, integrations, custom interfaces—gets built by asking Pi to modify itself, allowing non-engineers to extend functionality without coding.

bullets.text: Agent swarms creating technical debt that models can't process
bullets.summary: Interviews with 30+ engineering teams revealed code quality has declined industry-wide, with serious projects shipping what Armin Ronacher calls 'vibe slop.' Agents don't feel pain maintaining bad code like humans do, so they happily generate thousands of garbage lines; when codebases grow too large, future agents can't see all of it due to memory limits.

bullets.text: Claude 4.7 went literal; GPT-5.5 wants less hand-holding
bullets.summary: Anthropic and OpenAI both released new prompting guides that penalize vagueness in opposite ways. Claude 4.7 no longer infers intent and demands surgical specificity; GPT-5.5 treats step-by-step instructions as noise and prefers outcome descriptions. Both reward writing explicit success criteria before starting.

bullets.text: Anthropic and OpenAI deploy frontier models to cybersecurity teams
bullets.summary: Both companies rolled out frontier-model capabilities to vetted defenders, with Anthropic launching its service publicly while OpenAI restricted GPT-5.5-Cyber to critical infrastructure defenders. This move mirrors OpenAI's earlier criticism of Anthropic for similar restricted deployments.

bullets.text: Anthropic raising $40-50B at $900B valuation amid AI funding rush
bullets.summary: Anthropic is reportedly closing a major funding round valuing the AI safety company at $900 billion. This comes as labs race to scale compute and compete in the race for frontier models.

main_story.title: Why AI orchestration beats isolated automation
main_story.body: Michael Atalla, UiPath's CMO, sat down to explain why enterprises struggle with AI adoption despite heavy investment. The core issue isn't technology but coordination—organizations deploy disconnected AI agents and automations without linking them to business goals. Atalla draws a direct parallel to the Office 365 cloud transition: companies that failed didn't lack ambition but lifted-and-shifted without redesigning workflows. Today's AI leaders make the same mistake, fixating on model capabilities instead of asking how work needs to reshape. The organizations winning at AI treat agents as workflow components within governed systems, not standalone tools. Atalla also addressed job anxiety frankly: entry-level roles are being reshaped, but routine work transforms rather than vanishes. New roles emerge around workflow design and AI governance, though the skills demanded are different.
main_story.why: With 70-80% of AI initiatives stalling in pilots, understanding why failures happen matters for any enterprise betting on AI transformation. Atalla's insights, grounded in his 15-year experience moving enterprises through the cloud shift, offer a practical framework for what actually works.
main_story.take: The most refreshing part of Atalla's message: AI wins when treated as a workflow redesign problem, not a technology problem. Companies chasing full autonomy miss the more grounded and achievable near-term opportunity—agents handling ambiguous data inside orchestrated workflows, with humans keeping accountability.

around_the_horn.text: Three-quarters of AI experts optimistic about jobs, but only 23% of public agrees
Nearly half of organizations call AI a disappointing investment despite heavy spending
Atalla prefers solving the clarity gap between AI expectations and actual capabilities
